main_summary.summary: Charles McLaurin, born December 25, 1939 in Jackson, Mississippi, was interviewed by Emilye Crosby on December 5, 2015 in Indianola, Mississippi. McLaurin recounts his childhood in segregated Jackson, his grandmother's instruction in Booker T. Washington and W.E.B. Du Bois, early racial incidents at the train station and at a Rankin County furniture store, and his 1955 six-month active-duty Army training at Fort Jackson, South Carolina. He describes his 1961 recruitment by Rev. James Bevel after a Georgetown pool-hall meeting, his arrest at the Mississippi State Fair, Medgar Evers's intervention to bail him out, and his August 1962 trip to the Delta with Bob Moses, where he was assigned to Ruleville under the host family of Joe and Rebecca McDonald. He recounts meeting Fannie Lou Hamer in Tallahatchie County, escorting her to the Atlantic City SNCC conference at Fisk University, serving as her campaign manager in her 1964 MS-2 congressional run, and the 1964 Freedom Summer experience with northern volunteers including Tracy Sugarman. He concludes with his role in the 1966 Meredith March against Fear in Greenwood (where Stokely Carmichael's "black power" speech was delivered), and his stewardship of Hamer's 1977 funeral arrangements.
main_summary.historical_significance:
McLaurin offers a first-person SNCC field-organizer account spanning the Mississippi Delta voter-registration project from August 1962 through Hamer's 1977 death, naming dozens of local Ruleville and Sunflower County figures (Joe McDonald, Mary Tucker, Lafayette Surney, the Sisson family, Tommy Johnson) whose participation is otherwise sparsely documented. He provides eyewitness accounts of the August 31, 1962 bus to Indianola where Hamer first attempted registration, the September 1962 night-rider attack on Ruleville (Williams Chapel M.B. Church, McDonald and Tucker homes), Joe McDonald's defiance of the Ruleville mayor over his confiscated gun, and the Greenwood Black Power rally in June 1966.

The interview also records McLaurin's three-trip US Supreme Court "breach of the peace" case originating from Greenville, his work as Hamer's campaign manager when she filed for MS-2 congressional candidacy, and the disputed Atlantic City compromise of August 1964. His detailed recounting of negotiating Hamer's burial site at Ruleville (including Cleve McDowell's legal work, Henry Espy's funeral arrangements, MACE/Delta Foundation's financing, and Owen Brooks's coordination) is a primary-source record of the Fannie Lou Hamer memorial site's establishment.

chapters.title: Childhood in Segregated Jackson
chapters.summary: McLaurin describes being born December 25, 1939 in Jackson, Mississippi to John and Connie McLaurin, his grandmother's role as schoolteacher and her introduction of Booker T. Washington, W.E.B. Du Bois, and George Washington Carver. He attended Smith-Robertson Elementary, Marisey Jones Elementary, and Lanier High School on Farish Street.

chapters.title: Early Racial Lessons
chapters.summary: McLaurin recounts three childhood incidents that taught him about white supremacy: a train station encounter where a white man accused him of peeping at white women, a store job where white men berated him for writing a phone order in front of a white woman, and a Rankin County furniture auction where a constable threatened him for wearing an Army shirt with a PFC stripe.

chapters.title: Army Service and Awakening
chapters.summary: McLaurin describes joining the US Army Reserve in 1955 at age 15, his six-month active-duty training at Fort Jackson, South Carolina under the newly desegregated military, his letter to Congressman John Bell Williams seeking discharge, and his return to Lanier High School having seen "beyond Jackson" for the first time.

chapters.title: Meeting Medgar Evers and the NAACP
chapters.summary: McLaurin describes hitchhiking from Georgetown into Jackson and being picked up by Medgar Evers, learning Evers's identity from newspaper coverage, visiting the NAACP youth chapter office, and the inter-school rivalry between Lanier and Jim Hill that drew him toward retaliating against white drive-by attackers before NAACP organizing redirected him.

chapters.title: James Bevel and the Freedom Rides
chapters.summary: McLaurin recounts Rev. James Bevel coming to the Georgetown pool hall in 1961 and challenging the youth gang to ride to New Orleans rather than fight each other. His friends James Jones, Ralph Floyd, Levin Brown, and Charles Cox accepted the challenge and were jailed at Parchman, where Jesse Harris was "stretched" for refusing to count off as "nigger four."

chapters.title: State Fair Arrest and Medgar's Bail
chapters.summary: McLaurin describes his arrest at the Mississippi State Fair after attempting to buy a ticket to the previously segregated fair, Medgar Evers posting his bond, and Evers drawing a circle around the Mississippi Delta on a state map to explain how voter registration would transform legislative apportionment.

chapters.title: Bob Moses and Entry to the Delta
chapters.summary: McLaurin describes meeting Lawrence Guyot at the SNCC Freedom House at 714 Rose Street, his expectations of Bob Moses as a "Paul Bunyan type," and the August 1962 night drive to Greenwood with Moses, James Jones, and Landy McNair. They met Sam Block and Wazir Peacock in Greenwood after the White Citizens Council had run them out of Barnes Studio.

chapters.title: Amzie Moore and the Ruleville Assignment
chapters.summary: McLaurin describes arriving at Amzie Moore's house at 614 Chrisman Avenue in Cleveland, learning Moore's biography of NAACP organizing under cover of his choir's church visits and Aaron Henry's banking work via Tri-State Bank of Memphis. The next morning at Mt. Galilee Baptist Church, Moses introduced McLaurin, Charles Cobb, and Landy McNair to Joe and Rebecca McDonald, who became their host family in Ruleville.

chapters.title: First Voter Registration Trips
chapters.summary: McLaurin describes the Ruleville mayor (Charles Dorrough) arresting him, Cobb, and McNair shortly after arrival, escorting the three to city hall and back, and the first voter registration trip to Indianola with two older women driven by Tommy Johnson. He describes his transformative moment at the Indianola courthouse confronting the fear of lynching.

chapters.title: Fannie Lou Hamer Joins the Movement
chapters.summary: McLaurin describes the August 31, 1962 bus trip from Ruleville to Indianola with 18 registrants including Fannie Lou Hamer, the bus driver's arrest for the bus being "too yellow," and Hamer's confrontation with plantation owner W.D. Marlow where she told him "I didn't go for you. I went for myself."

chapters.title: Night Riders and Joe McDonald's Stand
chapters.summary: McLaurin recounts the September 1962 night-rider attack on Ruleville that shot up the McDonald home, the Sissle home (wounding two Jackson State students), and Mary Tucker's home where Hamer was staying. The Ruleville mayor confiscated Joe McDonald's gun the next day; McDonald, with Charles Cobb's assistance citing the Second Amendment, retrieved it from city hall in defiance.

chapters.title: Finding Fannie Lou Hamer in Tallahatchie
chapters.summary: McLaurin describes Bob Moses assigning him to locate Hamer in Tallahatchie County (Cascilla) where she had taken refuge. After getting lost, getting his car stuck in a ditch, and being pulled out by a white farmer, he found Hamer in a plantation shack and drove her to Tougaloo College, where she joined a SNCC delegation traveling to Fisk University in Nashville.

chapters.title: Freedom Summer 1964
chapters.summary: McLaurin describes initial opposition to bringing northern white volunteers to the Delta, his change of view after seeing the relationships between volunteers like Tracy Sugarman and host families like the Williams family in Sunflower County, the canary-yellow car incident with Linda Davis driving Black children, and the discovery that white people and Black sharecroppers could form genuine bonds.

chapters.title: Mock Election and MFDP Congressional Run
chapters.summary: McLaurin describes the Freedom Vote with Aaron Henry for governor, Rev. Ed King for lieutenant governor, and Hamer, Victoria Gray Adams, and Annie Devine for Congress. He recounts being summoned to take Hamer to the Mississippi Secretary of State's office to legally qualify for MS-2 Congress, paying the $500 filing fee via COFO, and reluctantly signing as her campaign manager when no one else arrived.

chapters.title: Greenville Courtroom and Supreme Court Case
chapters.summary: McLaurin describes desegregating the Greenville municipal courtroom by sitting on the empty white side, his arrest for "making statements calculated to provoke a breach of the peace," and his case's three trips to the US Supreme Court between 1963 and 1966-67. Police Chief Burnley arranged his quiet release on condition of one-year absence; R. Jess Brown was his attorney.
related_events: Burnley as later mayor of Greenville

chapters.title: Atlantic City Compromise Rejection
chapters.summary: McLaurin describes the MFDP delegation's rejection of the two-at-large-seats compromise at the 1964 Atlantic City Democratic Convention, the Lyndon B. Johnson administration's orchestration via Hubert H. Humphrey, Walter Mondale, Joseph Rauh Jr., Walter Reuther, Aaron Henry, and Rev. Ed King, and the post-1964 realignment of the white South into the Republican Party.

chapters.title: Burying Fannie Lou Hamer
chapters.summary: McLaurin describes his hospital promise to Hamer not to bury her on a plantation, his negotiation with Maxwell Funeral Home in Greenwood and switch to Henry Espy's funeral home, Cleve McDowell's legal work to establish the burial site within Ruleville city limits, Owen Brooks of the Delta Ministry coordinating the funeral, and MACE/Delta Foundation under Charles Bannerman paying the nearly $10,000 cost.

chapters.title: Meredith March and Black Power
chapters.summary: McLaurin describes James Meredith's June 1966 March Against Fear from the Tennessee state line, Meredith's shooting in ambush, the continuation by King, Floyd McKissick, and Stokely Carmichael, and the Greenwood encampment at the Black park after Mississippi Valley State College President J.H. White refused the campus. McLaurin was at Carmichael's side when Carmichael delivered the "Black Power" speech, which Willie Ricks had been chanting earlier.

loc_video.caption: Charles McLaurin oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Indianola, Mississippi, 2015 December 05
